title: Why I didn't sign the "We Must Act Now" statement (yet)
gist: Noah Smith explains why he refused to sign the vague economist-backed 'We Must Act Now' AI statement, arguing its hidden agenda — Acemoglu-style 'steering' of AI toward labor-complementing uses — is unworkable since no one can predict a technology's labor effects in advance, and current employment/wage data show no sign of AI-driven job destruction so far.

themes.theme: The 'We Must Act Now' statement and its hidden agenda

insights.claim: Smith declined to sign the 'We Must Act Now: A Statement on AI's Transformation of the Economy' because it is deliberately vague (asserting only that 'we must act' without specifying any policy) and he fears being associated with unknown future policy proposals the same coalition may release later, since signers of a vague first statement will be lumped in with a more specific follow-up even if they don't sign that one.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A live signal that a coalition of economists and tech leaders is coordinating around AI economic policy; watch for a more specific follow-up statement or proposal from the same signatories, which will reveal the actual policy agenda.
insights.quote: We must do something. This is something. Therefore, we must do this.

insights.claim: Daron Acemoglu revealed he had a hand in revising the statement's text after organizers reached out to him, and would not have signed the initial version; he says he 'wholeheartedly' agrees with its call to 'build the incentives, guardrails, and institutions needed to steer AI in a direction that complements humans and benefits society' — language Smith identifies as the core idea from Acemoglu's book Power and Progress.
insights.speaker: Daron Acemoglu
insights.so_what: Confirms Acemoglu's 'steering AI' framework is now baked into a prominent AI-economy statement — a concrete marker for tracking whether this idea migrates into actual US AI policy proposals.
insights.quote: to build the incentives, guardrails, and institutions needed to steer AI in a direction that complements humans and benefits society
insights.locator: Why did I sign this statement?

insights.claim: No one can predict in advance how a general-purpose technology will end up complementing or substituting for human labor — James Watt in 1765 could not have predicted most applications of steam power, so he had no way of knowing whether it would create more jobs than it destroyed.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Core argument against any AI industrial policy premised on picking labor-complementing technology paths in advance — a durable historical analogy investors can cite when evaluating AI-steering proposals.
insights.locator: Could James Watt, in 1765, have predicted

insights.claim: Geoffrey Hinton, one of the inventors of modern AI, famously predicted the end of human radiologists within a few years, but instead radiologist hiring and salaries boomed once it turned out AI complemented rather than replaced their skills.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A concrete, falsified expert prediction investors should cite whenever a specific 'AI will replace profession X' forecast circulates — treat such forecasts with skepticism absent confirming employment data.
insights.locator: Geoffrey Hinton, one of the inventors of modern AI

insights.claim: Acemoglu's own empirical track record on predicting AI/automation's labor effects is poor: his paper claiming robot-buying companies hire fewer workers was contradicted by many follow-up studies, and his theoretical paper claiming AI wouldn't raise productivity much rested on arbitrarily assuming away parts of his own model.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Undermines the credibility of putting Acemoglu (or similarly-positioned economists) in charge of 'steering' AI development, since his own predictive record on this exact question has been weak.
insights.locator: Acemoglu himself has certainly not had a better record

insights.claim: A panel of experts tasked with deciding which AI research/innovation lines to encourage or discourage would be operating on gut instinct and 'quasi-science-fictional supposition,' adding noise to the innovation process (uncertainty for developers) while being ineffectual at actually protecting workers.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A specific, reusable critique of any proposed AI-steering regulatory body — useful for assessing the likely effectiveness of future AI industrial-policy institutions.
insights.quote: in practice, any panel or commission set up to speed up and slow down various types of AI will be simply adding noise to the innovation process, offering rewards and punishments essentially at random
insights.locator: Here’s what I wrote about that idea back in 2023

insights.claim: Smith cites collectivization of agriculture and Mao's 'backyard production' campaign as historical precedent for heavy-handed government interference in an entire sector's technological development crashing and burning, implying similar risk from AI-steering policy.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Sets a high bar of historical failure for large-scale sectoral tech-direction interventions — a cautionary reference point for evaluating aggressive AI industrial policy.
insights.locator: There’s certainly plenty of historical precedent for that

themes.theme: Current data shows no sign of AI-driven job destruction

insights.claim: Employment rates for both the 20-24 and 25-54 age groups are about the same as they were before ChatGPT existed, and the employment rate for young college grads — the group most feared to be hurt by AI — is also basically unchanged.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Directly contradicts AI-driven mass unemployment fears with current labor-market data — a reason to discount urgent AI-labor intervention proposals right now and to instead watch these employment series for a future inflection.
insights.locator: The employment rates for people age 20-24 and 25-54

insights.claim: One recent study (cited via Ara Kharazian) found that companies adopting AI actually hire more workers than other companies in the same industry, despite widespread expectation in the AI industry that their own inventions will destroy jobs.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Suggests AI adoption is currently a net-positive hiring signal at the firm level, relevant to evaluating whether AI-adopting companies are shedding or growing headcount.
insights.locator: In fact, one recent study found that companies that adopt AI

insights.claim: Smith argues that given AI's currently benign labor-market effects, 'inaction' may be the economically correct policy stance — we may not live in the best of all possible worlds regarding AI's economic effects, but any large-scale interference in AI development risks leaving average workers worse off than the status quo.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A direct, contrarian counter-position to the 'We Must Act Now' framing — implies investors should discount urgency-driven AI labor regulation proposals and price in continued light-touch policy as the more likely near-term path.
insights.locator: It might be, in other words, that the AI we’re building now

insights.claim: Smith distinguishes AI's economic/labor effects, where he sees no urgent case for action, from AI's security risk, which he explicitly agrees is severe and warrants action now.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Clarifies Smith's actual policy stance: not anti-regulation broadly, but specifically skeptical of labor-market/economic AI steering while supportive of AI security-risk action — useful for not overreading his position as blanket AI-policy opposition.
insights.quote: Yes, I agree that AI presents a very severe security threat, and we must act on that.
insights.locator: In fact, the surprisingly benign effect of AI on jobs so far
